repository_ssim: St. Catherine's Monastery of the Sinai
contents_tesim: The Christian character of the Arabic undertext is proved by the occasional mention of "the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit" (fol. 114r, 117v). The text seems to focus on moral or ascetic issues (e.g., fol. 70r mentions "pure fasting"; fol. 118r mentions "those who consort with males")
full_text_tesim: The Christian character of the Arabic undertext is proved by the occasional mention of "the Father, the Son, and the Holy Spirit" (fol. 114r, 117v). The text seems to focus on moral or ascetic issues (e.g., fol. 70r mentions "pure fasting"; fol. 118r mentions "those who consort with males")

The script is characteristic of 10th century Melkite manuscripts. Two diagonal strokes are used for punctuation. Occasionally, one can see a diacritical dot under the letter /q/ — a fairly common feature in Melkite manuscripts (see, e.g., J. Monferrer-Sala, "Once Again on the Earliest Christian Arabic Apology: Remarks on a Palaeographic Singularity," Journal of Near Eastern Studies 69.2 (2010): 195-197
